sourceEvidence.label: Monarch Safe current source, focused tests, and Electron QA

assessment.summary: Two structural opportunities remain: remove ambient OS authority from the key-owning runtime and bound plaintext/decoder authority with a streaming content pipeline.

nonNegotiables: Vault content and keys remain outside the shared Monarch HTTP API, Oscar, and module kernel.
Normal content operations do not launch external Windows programs or require cloud services.
Physical erasure limitations on SSDs, backups, snapshots, and copied source files remain explicit.
assumptions: Windows is the first supported platform.
No measured latency, memory, packaging, or compatibility budget was supplied.
The current file hashes identify the reviewed working tree because the base revision does not contain the Safe implementation.

opportunities.title: Remove ambient OS authority from the key-owning runtime
opportunities.summary: Move key and ciphertext operations from a same-user Electron utility process into a Windows-restricted helper with a narrow versioned protocol.
opportunities.diagnosis: The current dedicated process separates failures but still runs Node with the Windows user's ambient authority; renderer controls do not establish an OS privilege boundary.

evidence.claim: The desktop main process forks the Safe runtime as an Electron utility process and passes device/capability keys over a private MessagePort.
The key-owning runtime is a Node process with a serialized message queue and no source-visible Windows restricted-token or AppContainer setup.
A temporary local Electron 42 utility probe observed Node 24.17.0 without process.permission; the unretained probe requires revalidation.
Renderer network, popup, clipboard, write, and delete gates passed, but those checks do not constrain the utility process's OS token.

desiredInvariants: The process that holds the master key cannot open arbitrary filesystem paths, create child processes, access the network, or use unrelated user credentials.
Every privileged operation is represented by a versioned, bounded message whose resource identity is validated inside the restricted helper.
Closing, locking, or crashing the Safe shell terminates the key-owning helper and clears its owned key material.

options.title: Dedicated Electron utility with strengthened local controls
options.summary: Retain the current process model, capability gates, allowlists, immutable storage generations, and regression coverage.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: The private channel and main-owned capabilities narrow dangerous operations but do not remove ambient OS authority.
The same Node utility process retains the user's ambient token.
This option does not add a permission mechanism unavailable in the observed runtime.
The existing application-level gates remain directly testable.

tradeoffs.assessment: Capability and resource gates reduce accidental or renderer-originated mutation, while same-user OS authority remains.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Keep IPC allowlist and signed-mutation QA, then inventory every Node/OS capability reachable from the utility process.

tradeoffs.assessment: No additional process hop or serialization format is introduced.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Retain current unlock and 1/64/256 MiB operation baselines.

tradeoffs.assessment: The existing utility and whole-buffer allocation profile is preserved.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Record utility and renderer peak RSS for representative content.

tradeoffs.assessment: Current tests show serialized auto-lock and immutable generation recovery, but OS authority is not part of that result.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Continue commit-point and forced-close tests on the production Electron path.

tradeoffs.assessment: Packaging remains unchanged and current logs/QA remain usable.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Confirm existing desktop build and support workflow remain unchanged.

tradeoffs.assessment: No storage or IPC migration is required.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run full repository verification and Electron QA on every change.

residualRisks: A compromised same-user utility process retains ambient filesystem, process, and user-token authority.
Calling the dedicated utility an OS sandbox would remain inaccurate.

workPackages: Maintain local capability, resource, lifecycle, and regression controls.
acceptanceCriteria: Existing focused tests and Electron QA remain green.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Revert individual local controls only with equivalent coverage and an explicit boundary update.

options.title: Windows AppContainer or restricted-token Safe helper
options.summary: Move master-key and ciphertext operations into a minimal native helper with no ambient network, process, or arbitrary filesystem authority.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: Main becomes a broker to a restricted helper; main-owned user confirmation remains useful.
Key ownership leaves the ambient Node utility process.
The design no longer depends on Electron utility support for Node's permission model.
Existing renderer checks stay necessary and gain an OS-level containment layer.

tradeoffs.assessment: A correctly configured restricted token removes classes of ambient authority from the key owner; the broker and Windows kernel become explicit trusted components.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Capture the helper token/capability set and verify denied filesystem, process, registry, credential, and network operations on supported Windows builds.

tradeoffs.assessment: A versioned broker protocol adds hops and copies unless shared-memory/range transfer is carefully bounded.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Compare unlock, 1 MiB random read, 64 MiB sequential read, and lock latency to the utility baseline.

tradeoffs.assessment: A native helper adds a process and bounded IPC staging buffers, while potentially allowing the Node utility to disappear.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure combined private bytes and ensure backpressure limits in-flight buffers.

tradeoffs.assessment: Parser/runtime failures can terminate a smaller component, but protocol versioning and restart recovery become new failure modes.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Force helper termination at every commit phase and verify the previous authenticated generation remains readable.

tradeoffs.assessment: Signing, packaging, Windows policy diagnostics, crash collection, and version skew add support cost.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Prototype install, upgrade, repair, and diagnostics on the minimum and current Windows versions.

tradeoffs.assessment: The runtime protocol and ownership boundary change even if the encrypted storage format stays compatible.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Ship a dual-run read-only compatibility phase, compare results, then gate write ownership behind a rollback flag.

residualRisks: The main-process broker can still misuse any capability the helper accepts.
Administrator/kernel compromise and plaintext visible to an authorized viewer remain outside the helper boundary.
AppContainer/MSIX compatibility and safeStorage access require prototype evidence.

affectedComponents: new Windows helper and versioned IPC schema
acceptanceCriteria: The key-owning helper has no arbitrary filesystem, child-process, credential, or network authority
Crash-point tests preserve the last committed generation
The desktop shell can revoke the helper on lock/suspend
migrationNotes: Preserve the current encrypted format during the first boundary migration where possible.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Keep the current utility runtime behind a release-only compatibility flag until the helper passes data and lifecycle gates.

opportunities.recommendation: For the requested maximum isolation, use the restricted helper after a packaging/token prototype proves compatibility; otherwise retain the baseline without overstating it as an OS sandbox.

opportunities.title: Bound plaintext lifetime and decoder authority
opportunities.summary: Replace whole-file transfers and same-renderer decoding with authenticated chunks, range IPC, backpressure, and decoder components that cannot mutate vault state.
opportunities.diagnosis: Current size caps and editor limits reduce exposure, but file reads/imports and archives still allocate whole buffers, synchronous compression blocks the runtime, and Chromium decoders share a renderer that owns the request bridge.

evidence.claim: The vault caps files at 256 MiB and uses authenticated immutable generations, but read/write and gzip/gunzip paths are whole-buffer and compression is synchronous.
The renderer receives complete files, bounds editable text/HEX, and uses Chromium image/media decoding plus a structural PDF summary.
Real Electron QA passed image/audio/video/PDF/HEX policy checks and mutation/network gates on small fixtures.
Commit and integrity tests cover local correctness but do not establish large-file memory bounds, decoder containment, or range backpressure.

desiredInvariants: No file operation retains more than a configured number of authenticated chunks in each process.
A decoder receives only the ranges and metadata it needs and has no credential, mutation, filesystem, or network capability.
Lock or cancel invalidates operation handles, stops future range delivery, and clears owned staging buffers after the current bounded commit point.
Declared MIME never selects a privileged decoder without bounded signature and structural checks.

options.title: Retain whole-file operations with strict caps
options.summary: Keep immutable generations, 256 MiB operations, bounded editors, strict archive validation, and current Chromium preview policy.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: Strict bounds and immutable generations limit corruption but preserve whole-buffer allocation and synchronous compression.
Editor caps avoid full HEX/text expansion, while full plaintext still crosses into the renderer.
Current supported preview and bridge policies remain directly covered.
Existing tests protect commit correctness but need memory/latency benchmarks.

tradeoffs.assessment: Current caps and mutation capabilities constrain known operations, but decoders and bridge remain co-resident.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Keep MIME mismatch, malformed input, bridge-surface, and exact-limit regression cases.

tradeoffs.assessment: Small-file paths remain simple; synchronous archive work can still stall the runtime.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure responsiveness and lock latency during representative archive creation/extraction.

tradeoffs.assessment: Peak memory scales with complete plaintext/ciphertext copies up to the operation limit.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Record renderer/runtime peak RSS at 1, 64, and 256 MiB and set a release threshold.

tradeoffs.assessment: Immutable generation and auto-lock serialization tests preserve the prior committed version on modeled failures.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Add disk-full and process-termination cases around every commit point.

tradeoffs.assessment: No new parser service or protocol is introduced.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Keep current QA artifacts and error-code observability.

tradeoffs.assessment: The current manifest/blob format and renderer contract remain unchanged.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run the complete focused suite and Electron QA.

residualRisks: Whole-file plaintext copies remain in runtime and renderer memory
Synchronous compression can delay lock handling
A decoder compromise occurs in a renderer that can still invoke non-mutation vault actions

workPackages: Maintain strict caps and expand malformed-input and resource benchmarks.
acceptanceCriteria: Exact-limit operations remain bounded and current QA passes.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Revert only focused cap or parser-policy changes with equivalent bounds.

options.title: Chunked AEAD, range IPC, and capability-free decoder workers
options.summary: Use authenticated chunks and operation handles with backpressure; place format parsers/decoders behind read-only range capabilities and no vault mutation bridge.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: Chunked storage and asynchronous archive streaming bound allocations and keep existing generation commits necessary.
Range-only decoders no longer share mutation authority; UI editor caps remain useful.
Existing small-format QA becomes a compatibility baseline, not proof of streaming containment.
Existing commit tests remain necessary while chunk reorder/truncation/backpressure coverage is added.

tradeoffs.assessment: Plaintext lifetime and decoder authority are reduced; parser defects remain possible but have less authority and bounded input windows.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Verify worker/process tokens expose only read-range/cancel and run malformed corpus cases with network/filesystem/mutation denial checks.

tradeoffs.assessment: Backpressure avoids large stalls but adds per-chunk AEAD and IPC overhead; sequential media may improve while tiny files may regress.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Benchmark candidate 256 KiB, 1 MiB, and 4 MiB chunks for throughput, first-render latency, and lock latency.

tradeoffs.assessment: A fixed in-flight window changes peak allocation from file-sized to chunk-window-sized plus decoder state.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Require combined peak RSS to remain under the agreed threshold for 256 MiB sequential reads and archives.

tradeoffs.assessment: Cancellation and parser failure can be contained, while chunk journals, backpressure, and worker restarts add state transitions.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Exercise cancel/lock/crash/disk-full at every chunk and manifest commit transition.

tradeoffs.assessment: Per-operation tracing, worker crash diagnostics, format capability reporting, and corpus maintenance are required.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Define stable error codes and a redacted operation trace before rollout.

tradeoffs.assessment: A new blob version and range protocol require dual-read compatibility and staged rewrite-on-update.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Keep current blobs read-only, write new chunks only after capability negotiation, and verify rollback with mixed-format vaults.

residualRisks: Bundled decoders still contain native/parser code
Plaintext needed for the visible range remains observable to a compromised authorized display path
Arbitrary formats remain storage-only until a reviewed decoder is added

workPackages: Add import/read operation handles and backpressure
acceptanceCriteria: Peak plaintext allocation is bounded by the configured chunk window
Decoder components cannot call vault mutation, filesystem, network, or credential APIs
Lock/cancel stops future range delivery and current committed generations remain readable
migrationNotes: Preserve v3 read support until all active vaults have completed a verified migration.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Keep v3 read/write enabled behind a compatibility gate until v4 mixed-vault tests and resource thresholds pass.

opportunities.recommendation: Adopt the streaming worker design incrementally after resource thresholds are set; retain the current caps as tactical protections throughout migration.

openQuestions: Is MSIX/AppContainer packaging acceptable, or must the restricted helper support the current unpackaged desktop distribution?
What peak-RSS and lock-latency thresholds must hold for 1 MiB, 64 MiB, and 256 MiB workloads?
Should a failed recovery-key entry be limited per runtime, per launch, or for the vault lifetime?
Which additional formats justify a bundled offline decoder after the read-only range boundary exists?
